words: Harmonium music played by Mrs. Agnes Healy Jones; Reading "Mrs. Annie M. Healy, History
of Pioneers of 1870-1889-1894" written
by Agnes Healy Jones.
AGNES: "Annie Mariah Tierney Healy was born October 15,1871 at Thorncreek
(four miles from Genesee, Idaho and three miles from Uniontown, Washington).
At that time it was then Idaho Territory and Nez Perce County and later it was
divided in two counties and the second county was named Latah. She was the
first white girl born north of the Clearwater River. Her parents came to the area
in early Vo and was the first white people to settle North of the
river at Thorncreek. The Indian cattle and horses were plentiful and some were
vicious. Mr. Tierney built a small house and hideout. The Indians did not like
to have the white people coming in. The wild game was plentiful and was mainly
prairie chickens. They would set a trap made of a large box with a figure 4 underneath
and place feed there and so many would come in and the box would drop down.
After a year Mr. Tierney built a sizeable home of logs with an upstairs added.
They made their cedar shakes by hand and cut logs to build the house. Mr. and Mrs.
Tierney always had a welcome for all. They had a post office, not authorized, for
four years to help their new neighbors receive their mail which was delivered by
a man through Palouse, Washington on a pony and was carried in saddle bags. J.C.
Hanson came to settle in 1872. Michel Evitts arrived in 1872 and it was not long
when Jacob Kambitsch, the Leaches, Scharnhorsts, Bottjer, and Strieback came
to the neighborhood in 1874. The Tierney home became a stop over place for
people traveling from Spokane and Palouse, Washington to Lewiston, Idaho, and
Mr. and Mrs. Tierney always welcomed them heartily. They provided the first
post office in that region to accommodate their neighbors for four years, 1870 to
Mrs. Tierney was a courageous woman as were all pioneer women. She could
ride a horse as fast as any Indian Cayuses could run. She also rode horseback to
Lewiston and carried her baby on a pillow. In good weather she would forge the
Clearwater River during the year of 1872 and just before John Silcot built a ferry
to
cross. During the winter of 1874 sadness came to the family. Mrs. Tierney passed
away leaving to mourn her husband, three sons, and daughter. It was a bad winter
and the snow was deep. The remains were taken to Moscow, Idaho for burial near
the Little Chapel on the hill in the Moscow Cemetary.
After Mrs. Tierney passed on,Mrs. Strieback took care of Annie and from then
on she was placed with different people. She was with Mr. and Mrs. Jim Carney in
Lewiston, At this time the Indians were quite uncivilized. They used to
fish along the rivers where Lewis-Clark Hotel is now located. There were many
horror stories regarding the Indian in those days. During June, 1877,
Mrs. Carney woke little Annie and told her to get up quick, the Indians were coming.
Annie was so frightened she claimed she could feel the hair raise on her head. All
women and children were hastened to a church Main Street by men on horses and
any other means of transportation.fc Mr. Martin Foster, a friend of the family,
picked Annie up and kissed her and said, "Goodbye, I will not be back." He
climbed on his pony and rode away. He was shot and they also shot his horse.
Both were buried " and the marker is still there. This battle
was the uprise of the Nez Perce Indians in 1877. The Indians were hostile over
the white people occupying their land and taking away their freedom. This was
a bad battle but the white settlers won after a loss of quite a number of white men.
The Indians then headed toward Montana and away from Lewiston. Annie never
had trouble with Indians after that. However, when her parents first settled
in Thorncreek the Indians were quite bold but Mrs. Tierney gave them food
and got along with them.
The first settlers would go by team and wagon as far as Palouse, Washington to
get provisions and were often overtaken with bad weather and snowstorms. They
would sleep under the wagon. Dried apples, coffee and other items came in barrels.
Sugar and many items came in boxes and barrels. The sugar was brown in color.
Syrup came in 2 or 3 gallon jackets.
In 1875 the first school, a log house, was built. The teacher, a Mr. Monroe,
lived at the Tierney home and helped with the children. Annie, at the age of 11, went
by
team and.cart to the Desmet Mission School for Girls. She returned home at the
age of 16 and married Daniel Healy in Thorncreek. (1887) Mr. Healy had homesteaded
land west of what is now Genesee, Idaho. She endured many hardships and learned
to take care of stock and remained there five days alone while he went to
the mountains to bring the wood supply for winter. She drove horses well and
one time when driving two colts hitched to a hack on the way home from town,
the colts did all but run away. At that time there were no fences between the town
and farms. She had a wild ride over the hills with the baby, Agnes, in her lap.
Mr. and Mrs. Healy remained on the farm seven years. They rented the farm
and moved to the Grand Central Hotel (known at one time as the Owen House)
in Genesee, Idaho.
In 1893 and 1894 Genesee was the most thriving town with a population of 1500.
The railroad was just new. There was no rail service at Lewiston or up on the
Camas Prairie region. This brought all the business to Genesee. The Stage
Coach driven by Felix Warren or Mr. Meeker used the Hotel as its stopping
place. At times it was raining and cold and the wheel hubs were deep in mud.
The passengers would climb out weary and tired.
The fall season was so wet that farmers never completed harvesting their
crops. Mr. Healy moved back to the farm and got his usual round up ofhogs and
turned them to the green growing wheat stacks and that saved him financially.
They lived on the farm a few more years and then moved back to town.
All pioneer women, the same as my mother had no patterns to make clothing.
They cut by chart which was a stiff paper with lines and dots for marks
showing sizes to mark through cloth. They used tracing wheels to mark lines
to cut .. They had to style the dresses according to their own ideas. My mother
made all her clothes and baby clothes by hand for four years and then her father
presented her with a New Home Sewing Machine. She also knit a lot of lace for
trimming. She knit all the socks for the family, and crocheted and knit lace
trimming for all the childrens' clothing. She made butter and had the name of
being a fine butter maker.
Annie Tierney was a religious person. Her mother was born in Denmark and
belonged to the Danish Lutheran Church. Her father was born in Ireland.
Mr. and Mrs. Daniel Healy were the parents of four daughters, Agnes, Margaret,
Laura, and Ethel.
Mr. Healy passed away April 15,1933. Mrs. Healy passed away March 14,1953."
How's that for a story?

speaker: A
words: "When Tom Tierney came to Thorncreek district, there was nothing but

speaker: A
words: wild flowers and fine bunch grass and lots of thorn bushes along the creek, so they

speaker: A
words: named the district Thorn Creek. Tom Tierney built his first hideout, a small cabin

speaker: A
words: of logs and mud, and it

speaker: A
words: was put together with wooden pegs." That's still standing. A hundred and three

speaker: A
words: years old this fall. 1973. "The roof was made of hand-made cedar shakes.

speaker: A
words: They stored their belongings and provisions. They used tripods to hold

speaker: A
words: iron kettles over an outdoor fire to do their cooking. They roasted potatoes in the

speaker: A
words: fire built outside.

speaker: A
words: He built a second log cabin consisting of large room and one room

speaker: A
words: upstairs in 1871. The kitchen had a pantry and a washroom on one end, and the

speaker: A
words: little bedroom at the other end. The inside of the rooms were lined with rustic

speaker: A
words: lumber. This was considered a nice home for homesteader.

speaker: A
words: The Tierney cabin was a stopover place for many people who were on their

speaker: A
words: way from Palouse, Washington to Lewiston, Idaho. The pony express rider

speaker: A
words: brought mail in saddle bags. The Tierney place was known as Thorncreek,

speaker: A
words: Idaho Territory and for four years the new settlers came for mail. After

speaker: A
words: rs. Tierney passed away in 1874, the mail was brought to the Beeman place,

speaker: A
words: presently Nordby's home near Genesee, and the rest of the mail was taken to

speaker: A
words: Moscow or "Paradise Valley."

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

words: "Copied from the book First White Women Over the Rockies, by Drury:

speaker: A
words: The camas root was an edible tuber somewhat like an onion. This was one of the

speaker: A
words: main items of food for the Nez Perces and the other Indians of that area.

speaker: A
words: The camas gr^w here in abun dance and it is the principal resort of the

speaker: A
words: Cayouses (sic) and many other tribes to obtain of it, of which they are very fond.

speaker: A
words: It resembles an onion in shape and color, when cooked is very sweet, taste like

speaker: A
words: a fig. Their manner of baking them is very curious. They dig a hole in the

speaker: A
words: ground, throw in a heap of stones, heat them to a red heat, cover them with

speaker: A
words: green grass, upon which they put the camas and cover the hole with earth, when

speaker: A
words: taken out it is black. This is the chief food of many tribes during winter.' "

speaker: A
words: When this camas was dried they would grind it up - mash it up and then

speaker: A
words: they would make their bread out of that too.

speaker: A
words: "In the early days the Indian women would do all the driving while their man

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

words: the wheat. They cradled the first crop. It would be four o'clock before they would

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: A

words: about the shape of a barn owl. They would "cuckoo" in the morning and call just as

speaker: A
words: plain. There were also lots of squirrels. The children made pets out of lots of

speaker: A
words: young things, even chicken hawks. We had a buckskin cayuse and we would all see how

speaker: A
words: fast we could ride her up the hill.

speaker: A
words: In 1901 my grandfolks moved to Moscow and lived in the first brick house built

speaker: A
words: there. It was located where the Safeway Store parking lot is now situated.

speaker: A
words: I was the oldest of the 28 grandchildren.

speaker: A
words: My grandfather Tierney had good health right up to the last. He walked down

speaker: A
words: into his garden a few days before he died. He lived to be 94 years of age. (1924)"

speaker: A
words: He passed away August 17, 1924.

speaker: A
words: And this is about rny dad. Well, I guess I could put that too.

speaker: A
words: "My father, Dan Healy, came to San Francisco from Ireland and around the

speaker: A
words: Horn in 1870. He never missed coming into Genesee for St. Patrick's Day»once

speaker: A
words: in fifty years. He was small of build but did lots of hard work all his life. He was

speaker: A
words: wise in choosing the land he homesteaded.

speaker: A
words: My mother always used clean, dry grass hay for the ticks used on the beds.

speaker: A
words: We had floors in our home and mother always used to keep them scrubbed white.

speaker: A
words: The rooms of our home were lined with cedar.

speaker: A
words: My folks had cayuses and lots of hogs and cattle. They had selffedders for the

speaker: A
words: hogs and also dug wells for the water supply.

speaker: A
words: They would haul wheat to town for ground flour, and bran and shorts to bring

speaker: A
words: back for hog feed when the mills were built close to town. My folks used to cook

speaker: A
words: potatoes for their hogs if they had lots of spuds that year. They would put the spuds

speaker: A
words: in the vat with wheat over them, and cook them in this way.

speaker: A
words: I was three years old when my folks moved to Genesee. There were a lot of

speaker: A
words: saloons, stores and livery stables there at the time.

speaker: A
words: My father, Dan Healy, ran the Grand Central Hotel in Genesee. They

speaker: A
words: charged $5.50 a week for board and room at that time. I remember the drummers

speaker: A
words: coming in with their samples. They always kept a sample room where they would

speaker: A
words: show their merchandise. They would go around through the country and also show

speaker: A
words: in the homes. Then they would send the orders back to the mail order house to be

speaker: A
words: filled.

speaker: A
words: There were two Chinese laundries in Genesee at this time. St. Joseph's

speaker: A
words: School was also started at Genesee, 1896, for the Catholic children. The first

speaker: A
words: school of logs was built in 1875, and set on the Brunty place now farmed by Louis

speaker: A
words: Hermann. Later it was moved within a quarter of a mile from Tierney's and it was

speaker: A
words: close for settlers who had children in the Thorn Creek District.

speaker: A
words: The Genesee valley, about 7 miles east of Genesee, was called "Cow Creek"

speaker: A
words: in those days.

speaker: A
words: J.C. Graves and son Clyde built the .electric train to Moscow in the early days."

speaker: A
words: And after the big train wreck in 1909 and 1910, why they sold out to the Great

speaker: A
words: Northern. Now its only freight hauling now.

speaker: A
words: "The railroad came first to Genesee in 1888. The old town was built in the

speaker: A
words: east end. Enormous prices were paid for the land for the railroad, so they winched

speaker: A
words: some of the buildings over to this side at the time the railroad was built. When the

speaker: A
words: railroad was built into Lewiston in 1898, it marked the beginning of the end for much

speaker: A
words: of the shipping of livestock and grains from Genesee.

speaker: A
words: The stage coach would come from Lewiston to Genesee, on to Moscow and then up

speaker: A
words: into the Palouse. Ezra Meeker was one of the stage coach drivers of that time.

speaker: A
words: I remember seeing the stage coaches go by. Once when they stopped, I

speaker: A
words: remember seeing a lady with a bird cage, and the bird inside it. I was quite

speaker: A
words: fascinated with it at the time, and never forgot it.

speaker: A
words: Felix Warren ran a stage coach through Genesee in 1893, during the time when

speaker: A
words: my father ran the Grand Central Hotel. Mr. Warren was a good fellow with a whip

speaker: A
words: and in handling his team of horses. It was often said he could "flick a flea off a

speaker: A
words: dog's tail." His sister was Mrs. Mary Spalding.

speaker: A
words: The stage coaches changed horses at the Smith place, north of town. Felix

speaker: A
words: wore a long rain coat that would shed water and a large hat. Lots of times there was

speaker: A
words: rain and deep mud to drive through. Felix Warren died at Almota, Washington

speaker: A
words: at the age of 85.

speaker: A
words: The Half-way House was run by the Michael Ruddy family for 2 years. At his

speaker: A
words: death Dick Ruddy and his mother took charge. Mrs. Michael Ruddy was left alone with

speaker: A
words: her children to raise. Later Dick Ruddy lived in Genesee and had two grain tanks and

speaker: A
words: a little house. He owned the livery stable at the time.

speaker: A
words: A team of horses for hauling would bring a big price in those days. Folks

speaker: A
words: would often stop overnight at the Half-way House, 5 miles south of Uniontown.

speaker: A
words: They would often hire a team to take them to where they wanted to go, after spending

speaker: A
words: the night."

speaker: A
words: ('''added to the conclusion of the written story: " James C.Hanson came to

speaker: A
words: Lewiston in 1872. Walked up the Lewiston Hill carrying their baby, stayed with

speaker: A
words: Tom Tierneys until they could decide on where to take up land. Came to Genesee -

speaker: A
words: John Luedke has part of the property. Mrs. Hanson and Mrs. Tierney were sisters.")

words: I've got some more to put on there. You can see type writing but I haven't

speaker: A
words: got it on there yet. But that's about all I've got right now.

speaker: A

speaker: A

speaker: R
words: .. .to the states?

speaker: A

speaker: A
words: (Reading: Thomas Tierney, from History of North Idaho page 227)

speaker: A
words: "Thomas Tierney. Few men have passed a more eventful and varied career than

speaker: A
words: the subject of this sketch, a son of the Emerald Isle, and possessing all the fire,

speaker: A
words: energy, skill, aagacity and brightness characteristic of his race, which has been

speaker: A
words: manifested in various channels of his stirring walk through some of the most exciting

speaker: A
words: regions of the world, the western part of the United States in the last fifty years.

speaker: A
words: Our

speaker: A
words: subject came to light of day on May 1,1836, in county Galway, Ireland, being the

speaker: A
words: son of Martin and Mary (Fahy) Tierney. He was educated until eleven years in his

speaker: A
words: native land and then came with an uncle to America where he attended school for

speaker: A
words: some years more and at the age of eighteen started for himself. He commenced

speaker: A
words: operation by working in a brickyard, then worked on the Erie canal during the

speaker: A
words: time of the last enlargement of that waterway. Two years at that and then we

speaker: A
words: find him in Iowa, later in St. Louis, then in Mexico, laboring at various occupations,

speaker: A
words: then in the time of the gold stampede to Pikes Peak he was with the first. After

speaker: A
words: the excitement he went to Salt Lake, driving cattle, thence to Kansas, where he

speaker: A
words: farmed a time and then he hired out to ride the famous Pony Express, riding from

speaker: A
words: Marysville, Kansas, to Big Sandy, No. 3. Six months sufficed him in this

speaker: A
words: dangerous and stirring occupation, then for one year he was at the no less hazardous

speaker: A
words: work of herding horses in that country.

speaker: A
words: Next we find young Tierney freighting from

speaker: A
words: Kansas City to Colorado, and his energy was manifest in that he gained twenty-five

speaker: A
words: thousand dollars in this business, which after the war, however, depreciated

speaker: A
words: nearly fifty per cent. At this time he went into partnership and lost the major

speaker: A
words: portion of his hard earned money. It was a great lesson and he collected his

speaker: A
words: remnants together and came west to Nevada, thence to California, on to Oregon and

speaker: A
words: finally landed in Lewiston in 1870. After spending seven thousand more in these

speaker: A
words: trips he went prospecting, then bought a team and hauled wood, and finally came

speaker: A
words: to the place where he now lives and squatted on a quarter section of land, which he

speaker: A
words: homesteaded, then continued to reside there and improve the same from that time

speaker: A
words: until the present, purchasing additional pieces of adjoining land until he has four

speaker: A
words: hundred and eighty-seven acres of fine, fertile land, which is mostly rented,

speaker: A
words: Mr. Tierney retiring more from the ardous labors of the farm. It is of note that

speaker: A
words: Mr. Tierney was one of the builders of the first telegraph line to Ft. Scott.

speaker: A
words: In 1866 Mr. Tierney married Miss Maria Beck in Kansas and four children were

speaker: A
words: born to them, Thomas M., married and living in Ohio; Gerome, married to Nora

speaker: A
words: Butler and living in Latah county; Anna M., wife of Dan Haley, and living in

speaker: A
words: Genesee; William, married to Louise Evits and living in Latah county; Mrs.

speaker: A
words: Tierney died in 1873. Mr. Tierney married again in 1896 and in the same year he

speaker: A
words: was called upon to mourn the death of his second wife." Which was a marvelous

speaker: A
words: woman. "In 1898 in Howard County, Iowa, for the third time Mr. Tierney approached

speaker: A
words: the sacred altar, this time leading Anna Drew, and to them have been born one

speaker: A
words: child, Michael J. In early times Mr. Tierney was active and for a season served as

speaker: A
words: postmaster, continuing in that capacity about four years. He affiliates with the

speaker: A
words: Catholic Church, and is one of the leading men of his section, being possessed

speaker: A
words: of those happy qualities of genialty and real worth."

speaker: A

words: he have been a fairly older man by then?

words: Yes, oh, yes, he was a young boy - man then. That was from Grandpa's

speaker: R
words: That was his son who died?

words: Yes, Michael. Um-hum. He was a sergeant in the office. Let's see what else

speaker: A

words: came to light..." And his place was - he was a great Catholic man, Grandfather

speaker: A
words: was. And he always - his cabin there was a stopping over place for Father Cataldo.

speaker: A
words: And Father Cataldo and the Rev. Henry Spalding were the missionaries in the Indian

speaker: A
words: in the early days of the Indians. And they converted lots of Indians and they learned

speaker: A
words: to talk the English language. And converted the Indians in many ways. And so he

speaker: A
words: made Grandfather's house ^stopping place and he used to have services there.

speaker: A
words: And through him, why he helped organize St. Boniface parish in Union town and

speaker: A
words: my grandfather was one of the organizers there and he gave the first $75 dollars

speaker: A
words: and that was a lot of money for them days.

speaker: A
words: And Father Cataldo used to say services out on the prairies and everyplace. And

speaker: A
words: then my mother - he suggested that my mother go up to the DeSmet Mission. So

speaker: A
words: he took her up there on a little two wheeled cart and her little belongings and she

speaker: A
words: stayed there then for 4| years before she got home again and they took care of

speaker: A
words: her and learned her a lot of little nice things. And then Uncle Will - then he sent

speaker: A
words: Uncle Will, the youngest son, to Gonzaga College for several years. And that was

speaker: A
words: first organized in '86, I think. And he was one of the first students there. And that

speaker: A
words: statue of Mr. Monahan on Riverside Avenue in Spokane was one of his schoolmates -

speaker: A
words: classmates. I always heard him tell about that. See, he's passed on too.

speaker: R
words: Well, did he know Father Cataldo?

speaker: A
words: No - Father Cataldo, he was one of the first missionaries, you see. Grandfather

speaker: A
words: was the first one to settle over there.

speaker: R
words: Right - right. Well, did your grandfather know him fairly well?

speaker: A
words: Oh, yes, he used to stay there all the time when he through the

speaker: A
words: country, you see. And he would travel through the country and he had a one

speaker: A
words: horse cart, you know these little sulkies? And he would travel around on that and

speaker: A
words: went among the Indians and him and Rev. Henry Spalding - he was a Presbyterian.

speaker: A
words: And Rev. Henry Spalding was a brother-in-law to Felix Warren, and he settled

speaker: A
words: down here at the Spalding home.

speaker: R
words: Felix Warren the stagecoach driver?

speaker: A
words: Yeh. He was related to the stagfedriver by marriage. And he and Father

speaker: A

speaker: A
words: Indians. They learned to talk Indian. Father Cataldo was an Italian. And it was

speaker: A
words: through Father Cataldo and Father DeSmet that a lot of this community was built

speaker: A
words: up, you know, through those two priests, you see. And they helped build the churches

speaker: A
words: around this community. And so many of the old timers here helped build the churches

speaker: A
words: oh, Dad and a whole lot of them. And when they wanted the news about the churches

speaker: A
words: and everything, I gave them a lot about the churches here too, you see. And this

speaker: A
words: church, of course, up here burnt in 1950, I think it was, -the old building, the

speaker: A
words: first building. And then my dad helped build that and they hauled rock clear from

speaker: A
words: Colton-Uniontown to build the church and all things like that and so they -

speaker: R
words: Did your grandfather ever tell you how Father Cataldo looked or acted or ?

speaker: A
words: I seen him. He used to here when he was a little stooped over man in late

speaker: A
words: years when I went to school in Genesee. Why, they had a - what do you call that? They

speaker: A
words: call it a - retreat or something and he was a lot of other priests. So out of

speaker: A
words: curiosity, I got to go up there and saw this stooped over little old man and he

speaker: A
words: spoke in Latin and everything and he could hardly talk. And I remember him. And he

speaker: A
words: helped build the church in Lewiston, Idaho, St. Stanilaus Church. He built that too.

speaker: A
words: And he worked hard among the Indians and he slept out under quilts out on the prairie

speaker: A
words: and everything. And he was a great friend of the Indians.

speaker: R
words: He just traveled around by himself?

speaker: A
words: Oh, yes, with his little cart and he'd stay with the people - what few people

speaker: A
words: there were, you know. And them days people had cabins and homemade beds and

speaker: A
words: everything, and be glad enough to sleep on the floor just so they had a bed to sleep

speaker: A
words: on,

speaker: A
words: you know. And the Indian cattle were ferocious,you know. Awful bad. And Grandfather,

speaker: A
words: you know, his place was always - he always welcomed anybody. And anytime of the

speaker: A
words: night or anytime, they always kept their door locked, you know, and they would have

speaker: A
words: to give full identification before they could enter the house.

speaker: R
words: Was that because of the Indians?

speaker: A
words: Oh, the Indians were ferocious,you know. The Indian might break in on you and

speaker: A
words: just take everything you got and I know my mother told me one time - she used to tell

speaker: A
words: me so much history about the country, you know. And during the Indian war, why

speaker: A
words: the Indians killed all one family down at Nezperce someplace there when they were

speaker: A
words: the war. And they killed all the family and one girl - she was left tied to a post

speaker: A
words: and there was a dagger driven through her tongue. And they took and they saved her,

speaker: A
words: the people did. And they trimmed her tongue off around. And for a long time she used

speaker: A

speaker: A
words: And they used to have her at the fair there and she had long brown hair and

speaker: A
words: she was nice looking. She could talk after her tongue was trimmed.

speaker: A
words: And the Indians used to fish along the Snake River there at Lewiston, you know.

speaker: A
words: And Mr. Damis, he used to have a store there and Amy Damis was his daughter and

speaker: A
words: there and all they'd wear was a piece of canvas - or a piece of buckskin around

speaker: A
words: their body, you know. So Amy Damis used to tease the old squaw Indians and all

speaker: A
words: and they'd just take Amy by the neck and throw her out and scare my mother nearly

words: to death. And they used to catch salmon there and everything like that and my

speaker: A

speaker: A
words: Idaho, and you see a street up there named Deakin on the agriculture side. Jim

speaker: A

speaker: A
words: And Grandfather left mother there and while she was staying there she fell back

speaker: A
words: in a pot of boiling brine. And she cried for six weeks. It was terrible. And the

speaker: A
words: neighbors, the Snow brothers, up there the Snows - it was theff*grandmother or

speaker: A
words: great-grandmother. And all the time they were trying to help poor Mrs. Deakin

words: all they could and for six weeks my mother suffered. So she wasn't making

words: that time.

speaker: A
words: "The paper presented by John Naylor, President of Latah County Pioneers, 1920,

speaker: A
words: throws much light on early settlers of this county, its formation, its first officers,

speaker: A
words: and history of Moscow, the County Seat. Moscow at this time has a population of

speaker: A
words: 4,200 and is the seat of the University of Idaho, the state's largest educational

speaker: A
words: institution. Mr. Naylor was an early settler of Latah County and Chairman of the

speaker: A
words: first board of County Commissioners. He has kept a careful record of chronological

speaker: A
words: events in this county.

speaker: A
words: His paper follows: As to who were the first settlers of Latah County, it would be

speaker: A
words: a matter of

speaker: A
words: cpngesture for miners and trappers were here for different periods before any

speaker: A
words: (sic) attempt at real settlement began. John H. Buchanan settled on Moscow Creek in

speaker: A
words: August, 1865."

speaker: A
words: And my grandfather - he was my grandfather's great friend, this Mr. Buchanan.

speaker: A
words: "He raised vegetables, hogs, chickens, and marketed them at Lewiston and

speaker: A
words: supplied miners who were prospecting or washing gold in the Hoodoo on upper

speaker: A
words: Palouse River. In 1865 Frank Points, Lad Miller, Aurthur (sic) Green, Charley

speaker: A
words: Hatter, William King, and Long Jim Lockridge were in the northern part of Latah

speaker: A
words: County.

speaker: A
words: Thomas Tierney and family settled in Thorncreek in September, 1870. At

speaker: A
words: that time there were no white settlers near him. The Indians were friendly and used

speaker: A
words: to dig their winter supply of Camas on the flat lands where it grew in abundance

speaker: A
words: throughout the county. Early in 1871 and 1872 the actu -al settlers who located

speaker: A
words: in around Moscow began to come in.

speaker: A
words: The sod was easily broken and produced a good crop with little breaking. There

speaker: A
words: was little money in circulation before that time and wheat became the medium of

speaker: A
words: exchange. After wheat growing became an industry here, three bushels of wheat

speaker: A
words: was exchanged for a day's work in the h arvest field.

speaker: A
words: The medium of exchange was trade, store, and jaw bone. My old friend, Tom

speaker: A
words: Tierney, always tells the boys the way he made his money was buying on jaw bone

speaker: A
words: and selling for cash.

speaker: A
words: Provisions were not plentiful at Lewiston and Moscow. Mr. Tierney would

speaker: A
words: drive a team and wagon over to Palouse, Washington, for flour, coffee and sugar -

speaker: A
words: it was brown. It was hard to get dried apples by the barrel. He sometimes got

speaker: A
words: syrup in two or three gallon jackets. Oftentimes he was caught in a snowstorm when

speaker: A
words: he slept under his wagon at night. He never seemed to have trouble with the Indians

speaker: A
words: and Mrs. Tierney used to say, 'Give them food and we will have no trouble.'

speaker: A
words: The prairie chickens were very plentiful and other game. Mr. Tierney built

speaker: A
words: the first cabin and the present buildings are part of the estate and remain in the

speaker: A
words: family today. Tom Tierney welcomed all travelers in his home for shelter on their

speaker: A
words: way from Palouse to Lewiston for food.

speaker: A
words: The first school built of logs in 1875 set on the place where farmed by Louis

speaker: A
words: Hermann. They moved within a quarter of a mile from Tierney's and it was close

speaker: A
words: to where settlers had children. Mr. Tierney served on the school board many years

speaker: A
words: before moving to Moscow in 1901. He lived until August '24. He died at 84 years

speaker: A
words: old. He was active in the last, taking care of his garden and going to town."

speaker: A

words: to

words: that.

speaker: R
words: Really? I wouldn't think after working that hard all day there wouldn't be anybody

speaker: R
words: that'd be finicky.

speaker: A
words: They really liked their food, you know, they really did. We used to have to take

speaker: A
words: hot lunches out to the men, you know, at quarter time. But they don't do that no more.

speaker: A
words: And they had cook shacks and after they done away with the cook shacks then the

speaker: A
words: farmers' wives had to cook for the men, you know. And a lot of women didn't like

speaker: A
words: it but now a woman of today, if they to take a hot dish to a potluck dinner they

speaker: A
words: are nearly killed, you see. So they don't have those nice gatherings anymore, and

speaker: A
words: that's

speaker: A
words: just too bad.

speaker: A
words: Genesee was a population, it was a busy place. And I remember we had Chinese

speaker: A
words: laundries here, had two of them. And they had saloons - one saloon - let's see - two

speaker: A
words: saloons and one brewery, and Matt Kambitsch used to make the beer.

speaker: R
words: A brewery?

speaker: A
words: Yah, and he had the brewery and he used to make it out of malt barley.

speaker: R
words: Matt Kambitsch?

speaker: A
words: Yah, Matt Kambitsch. K-a-m-b-i-t-c-h. And he had the best beer in town and people

speaker: A
words: used to take a little five pound pail and go down and get a bucket of beer for fifteen

speaker: A
words: cents. And they used to do all kinds of things like - And we had restaurants here.

speaker: A
words: And I remember when mother lived here, I used to take butter downtown and sell it

speaker: A
words: for twelve and a half cents a pound, two twenty-five cent rolls. And then I'd haul

speaker: A
words: chickens downtown, nice big hens, twenty-five cents a piece, in a little wagon. And

speaker: A
words: I was only twelve or thirteen years old. I went out and made the sales. I got along

speaker: A
words: alright. And when we lived on the ranch, I'd walk in there two and three quarter miles.

speaker: A
words: And I'd carry a couple dozen eggs. And you could buy calico, five cents a yard, enough

speaker: A
words: to make a dress. And everything was cheap then , you know. And people now. . .When

speaker: A
words: we'd go inside the grocery store and you'd smell everything and it smelled so good,

speaker: A
words: you know. And they had the lutefish in bales, and they were never very particular

speaker: A
words: about anything. And old Jack Rosenstein had his store and he had a cave in the wall

speaker: A
words: and he used to hang his, what they'd call sowbell, up in the ceiling.And they'd have

speaker: A
words: the butter in a cupboard with a screen on it and butter, of course, draws the taste

speaker: A
words: of the meats and things like that. But people bought it and they never hollared or

speaker: A
words: anything. And there were lots of bachelors around the country too. And they were

speaker: A
words: always.. .And the Indians - they used to call it an Indian reservation,when I first

speaker: A
words: come back here I talked about the Indian Reservation. Well, people didn't know

speaker: A
words: what I was atlking about. Well, it was out there, a lot of squawmen and a lot of

speaker: A
words: other good families living out there, you know.

speaker: R
words: That's out towards Fix Ridge or what?

speaker: A
words: No, right out east of Genesee, several miles out from it. Now it's mostly all

speaker: A
words: people - this Indian land, you know, has been bought up with government relief and

speaker: A
words: things like that. But I went to school with some of those girls. And the Indians

speaker: A
words: are good people in this way, if they think you are their friend - like Mother always

speaker: A
words: used to say - if they think - that was her experience, that was at the DeSmet Mission,

speaker: A
words: was half Indian and half white girls and they had them in separate buildings. And

speaker: A
words: Mother said, "If they think you're their friend, they're good. But, " she says," Let

speaker: A
words: them find that you're not their friend - you look out! They don't like you." No. It's

speaker: A
words: awful. And when the - during the Indian war, the Indians were scalping people.

speaker: A
words: They'd just take off just a hair off of here, you see, and Mother said they had to

speaker: A
words: take that to the chief to prove that they got a white man. Oh, yah, they were

speaker: A
words: kinda mean. And scared to death of Indians but she used always tell us not to be

speaker: A
words: afraid of them. And so many of these new people come in here why from the Eastern

speaker: A
words: states, why you'd say "Indians" and they'd get so scared and so frightened,

speaker: A
words: make fun of them and everything. Why it was awful how they used to talk about them.

speaker: A
words: And droves of Indians used to go by here and they'd carry their paraphernalia and

speaker: A
words: go

speaker: A
words: through and go up huckleberry picking and gather up stuff for the winter, you know.

speaker: A
words: And it was nothing new to see Indians on the canyons gathering up all the wild roots

speaker: A
words: for feed for the winter. Andthen the Indians used to get the deer hide and, oh, any

speaker: A
words: hide they could get hold of and they would let it decay ever so long and then they

speaker: A
words: had

speaker: A
words: kinda a gadget - bone like - and they'd take all the hair off of that and then they

speaker: A
words: would

speaker: A
words: let that hide dry and they'd chew it with their teeth and make it soft to make

speaker: A
words: moccassins and things out of. That's the way they did. But you don't ever see

speaker: A
words: anything like that anymore.. It's getting to be so few of them, you know. I used to

speaker: A
words: like to watch the Indians. Dad used to go deal with 'em, though. He never had no

speaker: A
words: trouble with 'em. Get your pigs cheap from them, you see.

speaker: R
words: What kind of a town was Genesee? Was it a pretty quiet town or was it a pretty...

speaker: A
words: Oh, they had every rule here they had to live up to. They had the Methodist Church,

speaker: A
words: the Congregational Church, and the Christian Church, the Lutheran Church and the

speaker: A
words: Catholic Church and the Danish Church. And that church up in the valley there is the

speaker: A
words: oldest church building standing now.

speaker: R
words: That's the Lutheran.

speaker: A
words: Yes, that's the Lutheran. And that's an awful nice little church inside, and then

speaker: A
words: they had - This town was quite a - They had doctors here, two doctors and they had

speaker: A
words: a dentist and a lawyer, and a real estate man and millinflry store and two nice

speaker: A
words: merchandise stores that I remember of. And it was real nice. Awfully nice town.

speaker: A
words: Restaurants and now I guess - I don't think they even got a restaurant here now. I

speaker: A
words: think that closed up. And - we don't even have a drugstore. And we had a drugstore

speaker: A
words: and Dr. Korn and his son they built a nice building and the fraternal orders used

speaker: A
words: to

speaker: A
words: meet upstairs, you know. And old Dr. Korn had beautiful white hair and a white beard.

speaker: A
words: And he always kept his floor oiled. So a certain man went in there one time and spit

speaker: A
words: on

speaker: A
words: the floor in front of the counter. And he just walked right around the counter, took

speaker: A
words: a

speaker: A
words: piece of chalk and marked a circle around him, he was so mad. He didn't like it atall.

speaker: A
words: We had pretty good doctors at that time but now I don't — I was about twelve years

speaker: A
words: old, that one doctor, he operated on my one eye and cut ulcers out. And I just had

speaker: A
words: to take it. He only uses cocaine and I couldn't stand that now. No. I had an awful

speaker: A
words: time.

speaker: A
words: Everything was so free and so open and such a clean town. The air, atmosphere, here

speaker: A
words: was so clean and pure, you know. And not like it is now. And we used to have such

speaker: A
words: snow

speaker: A
words: storms here! Oh, it used to be so - they could drive over the hills and over the

speaker: A
words: fences and everything. And now I don't know where the snow went. One year we did

speaker: A
words: have a lot of snow. I've taken pictures of the ranch out there when we had a lot of

speaker: A
words: snow, and it was an awful lot of snow. When I was a little girl we used to go

speaker: A
words: out coasting all the time, we used to have lots of fun. I'd invite the school kids

speaker: A
words: out

speaker: A
words: there, you know, and they'd have tobaggon parties and everything. But there's no

speaker: A
words: snow now, much. Kids don't know what a good time is.

speaker: R
words: What'd they do for a good time when you were a kid?

speaker: A
words: Well, we rode horses and we went out sleigh riding and we cut pictures out of

speaker: A
words: books and mother use to - we were satisfied. And we'd go out and play, you know,

speaker: A
words: and things like that, and we had pets. And then of course, school and had activities

speaker: A
words: then. But I don't know, the kids now-a-days have to get 'em a car and they have

speaker: A
words: to race up and down. And I don't know what they are doing. We had a kid bell

speaker: A
words: here, and everybody had to be in by 8 o'clock and they were strict. And I remember

speaker: A
words: the old lamplighter that used toTnere. We had those posts on the corners, you know,

speaker: A
words: and they had the carbon lights, you know, and he ueed to light. And he used to

speaker: A
words: get up on a little stepladder and light those lights. And then there was a pound here,

speaker: A
words: they had back of the livery barn. And

speaker: A
words: anybody's cow get out, why he'd put the cow in and it cost a dollar to get her out.

speaker: A
words: That's the way they used to do. Oh, yeh they did everything.

speaker: R
words: Did your grandfather ever tell you why he decided to settle out here? Why he

speaker: R
words: would go so far away from everybody else to settle into a place?

speaker: A
words: Well, I didn't come out to settle, I didn't homestead or anything.

speaker: R
words: No, your grandfather.

speaker: A
words: Well, grandfather came out here because the country was so epen. And he

speaker: A
words: wasn't the only one. There was the Bottjers and the Scharnhorsts, and the Evitts -

speaker: A
words: Mike Evitts was next, he was in 1870.

speaker: R
words: But your grandfather was the first, wasn't he?

speaker: A
words: Yes, he was the first one to settle over there in Thorn Creek, 1870. He came

speaker: A
words: down from Moscow. And he started in here. And there was a deep creek, nice

speaker: A
words: clean water and there used to be crawfish and everything. I remember all the

speaker: A
words: thorn bushes was there. And then Battjer, above him there. And they all worked

speaker: A
words: together them pioneers and they were so accomodating. The pioneers were kind, you

speaker: A
words: know, to one another, and always helped one another. And my grandmother's

speaker: A
words: sister and her husband, Mr. and Mrs. Jim Hanson, come out from back east, from

speaker: A
words: back in Kansas where they settled first when they come out from Denmark. And they

speaker: A
words: walked up the Lewiston Hill and they carried their oldest baby and carried their

speaker: A
words: lunch. And they up the top of the hill and they hired a team at high price and come

speaker: A
words: over to her sister's place in Thorn Creek.

speaker: R
words: Her sister was... ?

speaker: A
words: Mrs. Thomas Tierney. And they settled there, for,oh, a few months and they

speaker: A
words: thought they liked Genesee country better so they came here and settled over here

speaker: A
words: in Genesee. And right on top .the hill you can see them trees and they settled here

speaker: A
words: and they lived here. And they had three sons and a daughter, and the daughter died

speaker: A
words: in childhood days. And my grandmother and she were sisters. And

speaker: A
words: she was always a great lady - like my grandmother - always willing to help others.

speaker: A
words: So Grandma Hanson always went out anytime of the time of the night or anytime when

speaker: A
words: she was called and ride bareback, horse, rain or sun shine, and help people on

speaker: A
words: maternity cases and sickness. And she was awful kind to everyone. She was a

speaker: A
words: wonderful woman.

speaker: R
words: She was kinda the midwife around here?

speaker: A
words: Yeh, she helped a lot. And so she and they lost their daughter then and

speaker: A
words: they settled over here. And then their house burned. They had misfortune, but

speaker: A
words: that didn't discourage them any. He homesteaded land here. And they belonged

speaker: A
words: to fraternal organizations and they used to drive down to Lewiston and stay

speaker: A
words: over night and attend all the parties and hav e a good time. Great Rebejfeis, you

speaker: A
words: know, and Odd Fellows. And they used to entertain an awful lot. And Grandpa

speaker: A
words: Hanson told me that they used to have the best times when they were young.

speaker: A
words: They believed in having a good time and not accumalathig a lot, you know. Well,

speaker: A
words: that was a good way to be. And lots of people.. .And they ussed to have these country

speaker: A
words: dances, you know, you've heard of them, you know, where groups would gather,

speaker: A
words: you know, and they'd dance till two o'clock in the morning and everybody would carry

speaker: A
words: grub along and we'd have a good time. Play harmonica and violin, you know.

speaker: A
words: People had good times.

speaker: A
words: And then they'd give dances here in Genesee, certain parties would give dances

speaker: A
words: in Genesee. And any girl going out of the during a dance hall with a fellow, they

speaker: A
words: were talked about. They had to stay inside. It was indecent, you know. And guys

speaker: A
words: would chew cloves to kill the whiskey smell on their breath. Oh, and then sometimes

speaker: A
words: they'd have suppers, some organization would give a supper, you know, two

speaker: A
words: bits a meal and our boyfriend would take us to the dinner, you know. Oh, we used to

speaker: A
words: enjoy it very much. We had good times and then, of course, I left here when I

speaker: A
words: was about seventeen and I went to work in Spokane. Then I worked up there until I

speaker: A
words: was

speaker: A
words: married and come down back here. It seemed awful good to get down here on

speaker: A
words: this black soil where it was so dry, clean and wholesome, you know. Because

speaker: A
words: the city life - I wasn't for that.

speaker: A
words: I liked animals and I had success with 'em. Milking cows and raising calves

speaker: A
words: and pigs and chickens and poultry and I used ducks and chickens and rabbits

speaker: A
words: and everything, you know. I did an awful lot of that. And then I had butter, I used

speaker: A
words: to make butter and I had customers here in town for years. I always had something

speaker: A
words: to

speaker: A
words: sell off of the ranch. And then I used to ship quite a bit too to Spokane, 'cause

speaker: A
words: I

speaker: A
words: lived up there and I knew who to ship to. And I'd write up there and ask, you know.

speaker: A
words: So it's changed quite a bit then.

speaker: A
words: No, I was always wanting to be up in front and take the lead in lots of things

speaker: A
words: and I got along all right. I liked it. We had good schools here. And I praise the

speaker: A
words: University of Idaho, they sure were nice to me. My daughter had allergies,you

speaker: A
words: know, and Mrs. Richie and Mrs. Neilson, they helped me. And she belonged to

speaker: A
words: years of 4H and she won a trip to Chicago. Well, so did that one Colton too.

speaker: A
words: They both got trips with the 4H Club. And Mrs. Richie said, "Ife a shame to let

speaker: A
words: that talent go to waste." So I worked out a plan for her with a diet and she was

speaker: A
words: glad enough to eat in the kitchen no matter what she had just so she had her diet.

speaker: A
words: And she helped work her way through school. Now sh'e one of the best teachers over

speaker: A
words: near - she's parttime teacher at the community college. And she has a little business

speaker: A
words: of her own, her and another lady and they make these here ski suits, you know.

speaker: A
words: And so they get together on that for a sideline and she goes down to Portland and

speaker: A
words: gets her materials through orders from the people that... .And she gets ten

speaker: A
words: dollars for each member she had and she had .ten or twenty and she made an

speaker: A
words: awful lot of money last year. And then the Bernina sewing machine company

speaker: A
words: asks her for a demonstration and she gets ten dollars an hour for that. You know

speaker: A
words: she's busy too. Awful busy. So that's the way young people do if they only get in

speaker: A
words: and do it, you know. Why.. .And 4H Club, I tell you that 4H Club is a fine

speaker: A
words: foundation for young people. I think ife wonderful, if they stay with it, never drop

speaker: A
words: out.

speaker: A
words: Of you've got to take the bitter with the sweet, but stay with it - that's

speaker: A
words: what I say. We had good leaders here too. And the girls did the work on the ranch,

speaker: A
words: helped me on the ranch, and everything, and took time for 4H - just always keeping

speaker: A
words: busy. That's what my daughter always said, keep them busy and they'll never get

speaker: A
words: into mischief, you know. So, she keeps her children busy too. So they have - So

speaker: A
words: I feel happy about my children and grandchildren. They all done all right.

speaker: R
words: One question I have here. I was wondering when you were talking about when Tom

speaker: R
words: Tierney built his first cabin as a hidout. Did he have to haul the logs for that too?

speaker: A
words: Yes! They had to go up in the mountains but there was timber clear in to Troy,

speaker: A
words: you know, and Moscow, lots of timber around here them days.

speaker: R
words: About how long a haul was it?

speaker: A
words: Well, they'd take their, what they called their grub wagon, and they'd take their

speaker: A
words: wagon and go up there - the team and the running gears of the wagon and they go up

speaker: A
words: there and hew the logs out and bring them down here. And Palouse, Washington was

speaker: A
words: the nearest place to get provisions what they couldn't get in Lewiston. Lewiston had

speaker: A
words: nothing there.

speaker: R
words: Was it just a couple of miles or five miles, or ten miles?

speaker: A
words: Oh, twenty miles, twenty-one miles. All of that. Oh, yes. People in them

speaker: A
words: days lived out in the open and that's what made them so tough, I think. Yes that

speaker: A
words: was it. So anyway, why - Then we had the churches here, you know, and Sunday evening

speaker: A
words: at seven-thirty

speaker: A
words: there'd always be churches, you know. So we used to go to - mother and us girls

speaker: A
words: used to go to - all the churches and hear the sermons, you know, the ones they'd

speaker: A
words: have on Sunday evening. And I remember one minister - he was an evangelist -

speaker: A
words: traveling around and he was telling about- you know, they used to roll cigaretts

speaker: A
words: in them days - and he was telling about a funny story. He said, "Now I want to

speaker: A
words: tell you folks something for your own good health'.' He says, "Don't chew tobacco."

speaker: A
words: And he says, "Don't smoke - that's injurious to your health and cigarettes are

speaker: A
words: casket nails." That was a good answer. And then - oh, he told about different

speaker: A
words: things, oh, I don't know.

speaker: A
words: And there's so many of these people I can count to back four and five generations.

speaker: A
words: And when they go to get married, I have to stop and think if they're any relation.

speaker: A
words: There were such big families them days, but now-a-days there'fi maybe be two or

speaker: A
words: three, maybe none at all, now, you know. It's just different. I seen all these

speaker: A
words: houses go up around here. I remember all of them. And so many of the older

speaker: A
words: people in that generation are gone. My schoolmates, there were 17 in the class, and

speaker: A
words: I can count three here and that's all, living.

speaker: R
words: Who are the three?

speaker: A
words: 'Ifate Baumgartner, Christina Jacobs and Mrs. Kluss - Mrs. John Kluss.

speaker: R
words: K-l-u-s?

speaker: A
words: Uh, s-s.

speaker: R
words: How was your family affected by the first depression - the 1893 depression?

speaker: R
words: Was that hard on you out here?

speaker: A
words: Well, my father and mother were - you see, Dad rented the ranch out and that's

speaker: A
words: how his renter couldn't pay the rent.And that's why he had to move back, you see,,

speaker: A
words: to run these hogs up and make good, you see. The renter couldn't pay the rent and

speaker: A
words: it rained so much that fall. That was an unusual year, it rained so much that the

speaker: A
words: stacks turned green. They couldn't get them threshed. And them days they put it

speaker: A
words: in stacks when they headed it. And they had a derrick table that had four things on

speaker: A
words: it.

speaker: A
words: And that was what a derrick - top for this Them days the threshing machines would

speaker: A
words: crawl between the stacks and then they'd have to have this derrick - no, the derrick

speaker: A
words: table would crawl between the stacks and the threshing machine beside that. And

speaker: A
words: the derrick table would have these derricks - you know, these forks, and they'd

speaker: A
words: have to be covered and then we'd load them up and then haul them up to the derrick

speaker: A
words: table. And then move it into the cylinders of the threshing machine and that's the

speaker: A
words: way they threshed their grain. And that's what they had those days. And, of course,

speaker: A
words: few people had a header then - a thresher them days. And they had to wait for the

speaker: A
words: thresher and the thresher didn't come in so they lost their grain. So Dad took the

speaker: A
words: ranch back again and so we moved back there and we stayed there for, oh, I don't

speaker: A
words: know how many years.

speaker: R
words: And that's when he turned the hogs loose in to the

speaker: A
words: And that's when he turned the hogs in to the green stacks. That's all that

speaker: A
words: saved him that time. He went in debt too. He had to go in debt, you know, to get

speaker: A
words: started over again. But he made it though. Dad was sharp and he was interesting to

speaker: A
words: to. And when he was living here in town why, I used to go up and keep house

speaker: A
words: for him for four winters, you know, send the girls to school. And I used to hear

speaker: A
words: him. People would come up and I used to hear him talk about these pioneer stories

speaker: A
words: and I could jot down a lot of it. And then he used to get in fights in earlier days,

speaker: A
words: you know, and some of the old fellows used to carry blackjack, you know what that

speaker: A
words: is? That's a buckskin sack with buckshot in it. And then when they'd get mad, and

speaker: A
words: get

speaker: A
words: drunk and have a fight, why they'd hit 'em in the face with this. That's what they

speaker: A
words: had. And I know by name, knew the people personally, that had those. Old

speaker: A
words: fellows, you know, that are all gone now, of course.

speaker: A
words: Oh, Sunday in harvest time, they used to have so many - as I say, they used to

speaker: A
words: come in and do their shopping on Sunday. That was the only time the stores would

speaker: A
words: all stay open. And you'd go pas^r the brewery and we'd go with our heads straight,

speaker: A
words: and usually all the threshers were in there having a good time, singing, drinking

speaker: A
words: and fooling around, you know. And then they'd all come home with a hair box

speaker: A
words: with little water melons, and have a good time. Then the next day why they'd all get

speaker: A
words: to work again. That's the way they would do.

speaker: A
words: The town was awful lively then. The merchants did land office business and they

speaker: A
words: always looked foreward to the people coming in.

speaker: R
words: You think threshing was the biggest time of the year?

speaker: A
words: Well, the excitement, of the year was threshing. Yeh, about harvest time, that

speaker: A
words: was the biggest time. And we'd always have to get up about 3-4 o'clock in the

speaker: A
words: morning to get the things ready for breakfast, you know. And of course, they

speaker: A
words: never worked a place .^a week, you know, or something like that. And of course,

speaker: A
words: we had our own cured meats and everything and we could get some from over at the

speaker: A
words: butcher shop. Dad always had a keg of beer for the threshers. He always hauled that

speaker: A
words: out. So...

speaker: R
words: For daily use or for when they got through?

speaker: A
words: No, for daily use, all the time. He'd have it for them, you know. He wanted to

speaker: A
words: treat them. And he had a nice orchard out there and we had all kinds of fruit and

speaker: A
words: that too. He planted a little orchard and we had everything out there. It was just

speaker: A
words: too

speaker: A
words: bad I couldn't stay out on the ranch longer. I loved the hills. But I couldn't

speaker: A
words: stand it no more. I had to quit. You can stand hard work just so long and then

speaker: A
words: you've got to quit when it's time. Well, when the girls had gone to school, gone

speaker: A
words: away, why -married and gone, I said, "Well, what's the use." So I left the ranch

speaker: A
